title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Leo Grant, who is a mountain biker.
audio_script: Interviewer: My guest today is Leo Grant, a mountain biker who also teaches beginners. Leo, how did you first become interested in mountain biking? || Guest: I grew up near some hills, but I mostly played football. One summer my cousin lent me his bike while he was away. [20]I went out on the forest tracks every evening and loved the feeling of choosing my own route[/20]. It was quieter than team sport and I liked depending on my own decisions. || Interviewer: What was hard when you started competing? || Guest: Fitness mattered, of course, but I expected that. [21]What surprised me was how much confidence you need when the ground changes suddenly[/21]. If you look too long at a stone or a hole, you usually ride straight towards it. Learning to stay relaxed took longer than building stronger legs. || Interviewer: You teach beginners now. What mistake do they often make? || Guest: Many people buy a bike before they know what kind of riding they enjoy. [22]I always advise beginners to borrow or hire a bike first, then decide what they really need[/22]. Expensive bikes are wonderful, but the wrong one can make learning harder. Comfort and control are more important than looking professional. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most about teaching? || Guest: I like seeing people improve, but [23]my favourite moment is when someone who was nervous at the start finishes a route smiling[/23]. They often say they nearly cancelled the lesson because they were worried about falling. Once they realise they can manage, they stand taller, and that confidence is lovely to see. || Interviewer: Have you had any serious accidents? || Guest: Nothing terrible, luckily. I broke a finger once, but that was my own fault. [24]The accident taught me to check the weather properly before a ride[/24], because a dry path in the morning can become slippery after heavy rain. Now I change plans if conditions are wrong, even for a competition. || Interviewer: What are your plans for the next year? || Guest: I'm entering fewer races. [25]I want to spend more time creating safe local routes for young riders[/25]. There are lots of teenagers with bikes, but they often ride on busy roads because they don't know where else to go. If we build better marked trails, more families will try the sport safely. || Interviewer: Do you still ride just for fun? || Guest: Yes, and I think that is important. When sport becomes your work, it is easy to turn every ride into training. I try to keep one ride each week where I do not time myself or think about improvement. I just go with a friend, stop for coffee, and enjoy the landscape. Those rides remind me why I started. They also make me a better teacher, because beginners are usually looking for enjoyment before they care about speed or technique. I also volunteer at local events, checking helmets and brakes before young people ride. Small safety checks sound boring, but they prevent accidents and help parents feel more confident about the sport.  I also ask experienced riders to slow down and encourage beginners more often.  Safety matters before speed.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: My guest today is Leo Grant, a mountain biker who also teaches beginners. Leo, how did you first become interested in mountain biking?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I grew up near some hills, but I mostly played football. One summer my cousin lent me his bike while he was away. I went out on the forest tracks every evening and loved the feeling of choosing my own route. It was quieter than team sport and I liked depending on my own decisions.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was hard when you started competing?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Fitness mattered, of course, but I expected that. What surprised me was how much confidence you need when the ground changes suddenly. If you look too long at a stone or a hole, you usually ride straight towards it. Learning to stay relaxed took longer than building stronger legs.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: You teach beginners now. What mistake do they often make?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Many people buy a bike before they know what kind of riding they enjoy. I always advise beginners to borrow or hire a bike first, then decide what they really need. Expensive bikes are wonderful, but the wrong one can make learning harder. Comfort and control are more important than looking professional.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you enjoy most about teaching?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I like seeing people improve, but my favourite moment is when someone who was nervous at the start finishes a route smiling. They often say they nearly cancelled the lesson because they were worried about falling. Once they realise they can manage, they stand taller, and that confidence is lovely to see.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Have you had any serious accidents?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Nothing terrible, luckily. I broke a finger once, but that was my own fault. The accident taught me to check the weather properly before a ride, because a dry path in the morning can become slippery after heavy rain. Now I change plans if conditions are wrong, even for a competition.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What are your plans for the next year?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I'm entering fewer races. I want to spend more time creating safe local routes for young riders. There are lots of teenagers with bikes, but they often ride on busy roads because they don't know where else to go. If we build better marked trails, more families will try the sport safely.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you still ride just for fun?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, and I think that is important. When sport becomes your work, it is easy to turn every ride into training. I try to keep one ride each week where I do not time myself or think about improvement. I just go with a friend, stop for coffee, and enjoy the landscape. Those rides remind me why I started. They also make me a better teacher, because beginners are usually looking for enjoyment before they care about speed or technique. I also volunteer at local events, checking helmets and brakes before young people ride. Small safety checks sound boring, but they prevent accidents and help parents feel more confident about the sport.  I also ask experienced riders to slow down and encourage beginners more often.  Safety matters before speed.

questions.question: What first attracted Leo to mountain biking?

options.A: being able to choose where he went
options.B: joining his cousin on long rides
options.C: training for football in the hills

questions.explanation: Leo says he loved the feeling of choosing his own route on forest tracks.

questions.question: What surprised Leo when he began competing?

options.A: how fit he needed to be

questions.explanation: He says he was surprised by how much confidence you need when the ground changes.

questions.question: What does Leo advise beginners to do?

options.B: try a bike before buying one

questions.explanation: Leo advises beginners to borrow or hire a bike before deciding what they need.

questions.question: What does Leo enjoy most when teaching?
questions.explanation: He likes it when someone nervous finishes a route smiling.

questions.question: What did Leo learn after an accident?

options.A: to ride more slowly in competitions

questions.explanation: He says the accident taught him to check the weather properly before a ride.

questions.question: What does Leo want to do next year?

options.A: enter more races for young riders
options.C: teach families how to repair bikes

questions.explanation: Leo wants to create safe local routes for young riders.
